narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70, which stands out well above the typical student's average of 3.21, and a respectable LSAT score of 33. Your family comes from a middle-income background, and you're planning to attend part-time, which is less common as most students go full-time. The admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 57.5% chance that you would fail the bar—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes those with stronger predicted odds of passing. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, far below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school raised significant concerns about bar readiness. On a positive note, your strong undergraduate GPA had the second-strongest influence, actively working against failure by demonstrating your college-level capability. Your LSAT score also helped pull the prediction toward success, though not as powerfully as your GPA. Being a part-time student nudged things slightly toward the failure side, as the model sees full-time paths as more reliably linked to passing the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 2 played a smaller role in favoring failure, while your middle-income family background offered a minor boost toward passing. Compared to typical students, your early and late school rankings lag behind averages around 5-6, which overshadowed your undergraduate achievements despite the model recognizing them. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—it's just how these patterns stacked up for you specifically. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and exploring full-time options if feasible, then reapply with an updated profile that addresses these key areas. You've already beaten averages in key spots—building on that could shift the odds dramatically in your favor next time. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
